---
title: "Global Temperature Anomalies in Practice"
subtitle: "An Open, Reproducible Framework for Baseline Harmonisation and ENSO-Aware Visualisation"
date: today
author:
  - name: J. Eduardo Vera-Valdés
    email: eduardo@math.aau.dk
    url: https://everval.github.io
    affiliations:
      - name: Aalborg University
        department: Department of Mathematical Sciences
        country: Denmark
      - name: CoRE
        department: "Center for Research in Energy: Economics and Markets"
        country: Denmark
    orcid: 0000-0002-0337-8055
    corresponding: true
abstract: |
    We present a reproducible pipeline for downloading, processing, and harmonising global temperature anomaly series from HadCRUT5, GISTEMP, NOAAGlobalTemp, Berkeley Earth, and ERA5. All datasets are aligned to a common 1850-1900 pre-industrial baseline to enable direct comparison. The workflow also integrates the Oceanic Niño Index (ONI) to annotate El Niño and La Niña phases in the figures, helping distinguish short-term variability from the long-term warming signal. Final outputs are exported as transparent, analysis-ready CSV files and made available through the repository.
keywords: [Global Temperature Anomalies, Pre-industrial Baseline, El Niño Southern Oscillation]
execute:
  enabled: false
  engine: julia
bibliography: library.bib
---

## Introduction

Global temperature anomalies are central to climate variability and change analysis, but practical use across datasets is often limited by inconsistent baseline periods and heterogeneous data formats. @tbl-baselines summarises the baseline periods, formats, and key notes for five of the most widely used global temperature anomaly datasets: HadCRUT5 [@HadCRUT5], GISTEMP [@GISTEMP], NOAAGlobalTemp [@NOAAGlobalTemp], Berkeley Earth [@BerkeleyEarth], and ERA5 [@ERA5].

| Dataset | Baseline period | Format | Practical note |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| HadCRUT5 | 1961–1990 | CSV | Release lag is typically longer than for the other datasets. |
| NOAAGlobalTemp | 1991–2020 | ASCII | Data is presented using space-delimited files. File names are versioned, so the download URL changes over time. |
| GISTEMP | 1951–1980 | CSV | Series begins in 1880, so an additional pre-industrial adjustment is needed. Data is in wide format. |
| Berkeley Earth | 1951–1980 | TXT | Distributed as space-delimited text files. |
| ERA5 | No fixed baseline | NetCDF | No global average anomaly is provided; users must compute anomalies relative to a selected climatology. Requires setting up a key for accessing the data. |
: Baseline periods and processing considerations for major global temperature anomaly datasets.^[Note: baseline definitions may change; always check current provider documentation.] {#tbl-baselines tbl-colwidths="[20,20,9,51]"}

The table highlights two recurring operational barriers: heterogeneous file structures and inconsistent baseline definitions. Together, these barriers reduce comparability, increase processing overhead, and weaken reproducibility in applied climate analysis.

This paper addresses these barriers with a concise, reusable workflow that harmonises all series to a common 1850-1900 reference and a shared tabular format. The implementation is written in Julia [@Julia] for clarity, speed, and straightforward reproducibility.

We standardise all datasets listed in @tbl-baselines, compute anomalies relative to the common baseline, and produce a unified analysis table. To support interpretation, we integrate the Oceanic Niño Index [@SST_Part1; @SST_Part2; @NOAA_ONI] and annotate El Niño and La Niña phases in the plots, separating short-term ENSO variability from long-run warming.

The remainder of the paper proceeds from environment setup and source-specific processing to dataset merging and comparative visualisation with ENSO overlays.

## Necessary Packages

To ensure reproducibility and ease of use, this notebook uses a Julia project environment with pinned dependencies. The environment is defined by `Project.toml` (and `Manifest.toml` when present), allowing others to reproduce the same setup.


```{julia}
#| include: false
#| echo: false
#| output: false
#| message: false
# Load the package environment and core dependencies
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(pwd())
using CSV, DataFrames, Plots
```

```julia
# Initialize the project environment 
using Pkg
Pkg.activate(pwd())
```
The code above activates the project environment in the current directory. The workflow makes use of several packages for data download, processing, and visualisation: `CDSAPI` [@CDSAPIjl], `CSV` [@CSVjl], `Dates`, `DataFrames` [@DataFramesjl], `HTTP` [@HTTPjl], `NCDatasets` [@NCDatasetsjl], `Plots` [@Plotsjl], and `Statistics`.  To ensure reproducibility, these packages should be added to the `Project.toml` file.

`Dates` and `Statistics` are included in the Julia standard library; the remaining packages can be installed with `Pkg.add`. The code snippet below shows how to add the necessary packages to the environment. This step only needs to be done once, and the resulting `Project.toml` and `Manifest.toml` files will capture the exact versions used for reproducibility.

```julia
# Install necessary packages
Pkg.add(["CDSAPI", "CSV", "DataFrames", "HTTP", "NCDatasets", "Plots"])
```

After installation, packages are loaded with `using`. 

```julia
# Load the necessary packages
using CDSAPI, CSV, DataFrames, Dates, HTTP, NCDatasets, Plots, Statistics
```

Before presenting each source-specific subsection, the workflow follows four common steps: (1) retrieve raw data, (2) parse and standardise into a common date-anomaly structure, (3) align to the pre-industrial reference, and (4) save a cleaned CSV for merging and plotting. This common structure keeps the pipeline modular and easy to maintain when providers update formats or endpoints.

## HadCRUT5 {#sec-HadCRUT5}

The HadCRUT5 dataset is provided by the Met Office Hadley Centre and the Climatic Research Unit at the University of East Anglia [@HadCRUT5]. The HadCRUT5 dataset is available in CSV format from the [Met Office website](https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/hadobs/hadcrut5/). The code below downloads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file. 
```julia
# Download the HadCRUT temperature data 
# URL of the HadCRUT5 global monthly average CSV
hurl = "https://www.metoffice.gov.uk/hadobs/hadcrut5/data/HadCRUT.5.1.0.0/analysis/diagnostics/HadCRUT.5.1.0.0.analysis.summary_series.global.monthly.csv"
# Local filename to save
hfilename = "data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv"
open(hfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(hurl).body)
end
```

The next code snippet reads the downloaded CSV file, renames the columns for clarity, and extracts the date and raw temperature anomalies. The code then calculates the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline and saves the processed data in a new CSV file, available at `data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv` in the repository. The last line shows the last 3 rows of the processed HadCRUT5 data.

```julia
# Read the downloaded CSV file and process the data
rawhadcrut = CSV.read(hfilename, DataFrame)
rename!(rawhadcrut, :Time => :Date)
rename!(rawhadcrut, :"Anomaly (deg C)" => :RawTemperature)
hadcrut = rawhadcrut[!, [:Date, :RawTemperature]]
# Calculate temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline
oldbase = mean(hadcrut[(hadcrut.Date.>=Date(1850, 1, 1)).&(hadcrut.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemperature])
hadcrut[!, :Temp] = hadcrut[!, :RawTemperature] .- oldbase;
# Save the processed data in a new CSV file
CSV.write(hfilename, hadcrut)
last(hadcrut, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the HadCRUT5 data
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview processed HadCRUT5 data
hfilename = "data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv"
hadcrut = CSV.read(hfilename, DataFrame)
last(hadcrut, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the HadCRUT5 data
```


## NOAAGlobalTemp {#sec-NOAAGlobalTemp}

The NOAAGlobalTemp dataset is provided by the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) [@NOAAGlobalTemp]. It is available in ASCII format from the [NOAA NCEI website](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/land-based-station/noaa-global-temp), where space-delimited files are used. Moreover, the URL of the dataset depends on the month of the data; note the `...YYYYMM.asc` name in the URL. 

```julia
# Download the NOAAGlobalTemp temperature data 
# URL of the NOAAGlobalTemp global monthly average CSV ---yearmonth.asc
nurl = "https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/data/noaa-global-surface-temperature/v6.1/access/timeseries/aravg.mon.land_ocean.90S.90N.v6.1.0.202603.asc"
# Local filename to save
nfilename = "data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv"
# Download the file
open(nfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(nurl).body)
end
lines = readlines(nfilename)
cleaned_lines = [join(split(strip(line)), ",") for line in lines]
# Write to file
write(nfilename, join(cleaned_lines, "\n"))
```

The code includes a cleaning step that converts the downloaded file to CSV format. The `cleaned_lines` variable is built by stripping each line (`strip()`), splitting it on whitespace (`split()`), and joining the resulting fields with commas (`join()`). The cleaned lines are then written back to the same file.

The data are then used to calculate temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline, and the processed data are saved in a new CSV file, available at `data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv` in the repository. The last line shows the last 3 rows of the processed NOAAGlobalTemp data.

```julia
# Read the cleaned CSV file
rawnoaa = CSV.read(nfilename, DataFrame; delim=',', header=0)
dates = Date.(rawnoaa.Column1, rawnoaa.Column2, 1) # Create Date column from Column1 and Column2
noaa = DataFrame(:Date=>dates, :RawTemp=>rawnoaa.Column3)
oldbase = mean(noaa[(noaa.Date.>=Date(1850, 1, 1)).&(noaa.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemp])
noaa[!, :Temp] = noaa[!, :RawTemp] .- oldbase
CSV.write(nfilename, noaa)
last(noaa, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the NOAAGlobalTemp data
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview processed NOAA data
nfilename = "data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv"
noaa = CSV.read(nfilename, DataFrame)
last(noaa, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the NOAAGlobalTemp data
```


## GISTEMP {#sec-GISTEMP}

The GISTEMP dataset [@GISTEMP] is available in CSV format from [the NASA GISS website](https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/). The data is provided in a wide format, where each row corresponds to one year and monthly values are stored in separate columns. To process this data, we need to convert it to a long format, where each row corresponds to a single month. The `longseries` function defined below performs this conversion.

```julia
# Function to convert wide format data to long format
function longseries(data)
    height = size(data, 1) # Number of rows, equivalent to the number of years
    last_row = 12 - count(ismissing, data[end, 2:13]) # Number of non-missing months in the last year
    many = (height - 1) * 12 + last_row # Total number of months in the long format
    long = zeros(many, 1) # Long format array
    for ii = 1:(height-1) # Loop through all years except the last one
        for jj = 1:12 # Loop through all months
            long[(ii-1)*12+jj] = data[ii, jj+1]
        end
    end
    for jj = 1:last_row # Loop through the last year
        long[(height-1)*12+jj] = data[height, jj+1]
    end
    return long
end
```

The function takes a DataFrame in wide format and returns a long-format array. It calculates the total number of months based on the number of years, same as the number of rows, and the number of non-missing months in the last year. It then fills the long-format array by looping through the years and months.

Once the `longseries` function is defined, we can use it to process the GISTEMP data. 

```julia
# Download the GISTEMP temperature data 
# URL of the GISTEMP global monthly average CSV
gurl = "https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/tabledata_v4/GLB.Ts%2BdSST.csv"
# Local filename to save
gfilename = "data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv"
# Download the file
open(gfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(gurl).body)
end
```

The data is then cleaned from the `***` string used to denote missing values, and converts it to long format using the `longseries` function defined above. The resulting long-format array is stored in the `gistemp` variable.

```julia
# Removing missing values and converting to long format
longgistemp = CSV.read(gfilename, DataFrame, header=2, missingstring=["***"])
gistemp = longseries(longgistemp)[:]
```

A date array is created based on the number of months in the dataset, starting from January 1880. The raw temperature anomalies are then adjusted to calculate anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. Following the data source recommendation, the data are first adjusted to an 1880-1900 baseline and then shifted by 0.038°C to account for pre-1880 conditions^[https://data.giss.nasa.gov/gistemp/faq/#q102a].

```julia
# Build dates and compute adjusted anomalies for GISTEMP
Tt = length(gistemp) - 1
start = Date(1880, 1, 1) # Start date of the dataset
fin = start + Month(Tt) # End date of the dataset
dates = collect(start:Month(1):fin) # Create a Date array
gistemp = DataFrame(:Date=>dates, :RawTemp=>gistemp)
oldbase = mean(gistemp[(gistemp.Date.>=Date(1880, 1, 1)).&(gistemp.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemp])
gistemp[!, :Temp] = gistemp[!, :RawTemp] .- oldbase .+ 0.038 # Adjust for pre-1880 data
CSV.write(gfilename, gistemp)
last(gistemp, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the GISTEMP data
```

The CSV file with the processed GISTEMP data is now saved in the repository as `data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv`. 

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview processed GISTEMP data
gfilename = "data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv"
gistemp = CSV.read(gfilename, DataFrame)
last(gistemp, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the GISTEMP data
```


## Berkeley Earth {#sec-BerkeleyEarth}

The Berkeley Earth dataset [@BerkeleyEarth] is available in TXT format from [the Berkeley Earth website](https://berkeleyearth.org/) using space-delimited files. The code below downloads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file.

```julia
# Download the Berkeley Earth temperature data
# URL of the Berkeley Earth global monthly average CSV
burl = "https://storage.googleapis.com/berkeley-earth-temperature-hr/global/Global_TAVG_monthly.txt"
# Local filename to save
bfilename = "data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv"
# Download the file
open(bfilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(burl).body)
end
```

A new variable is created to store the date, which is constructed from the year and month columns in the dataset. The raw temperature anomalies are then adjusted to calculate temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline, and the processed data are saved in a new CSV file, available at `data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv` in the repository. The last line shows the last 3 rows of the processed Berkeley Earth data.

```julia
# Read, rename, and baseline-adjust Berkeley Earth data
rawtemp = CSV.read(bfilename, DataFrame, comment="%", delim=" ", ignorerepeated=true)
colnames = [:Year, :Month, :Anomaly_Monthly, :Unc_Monthly,
            :Anomaly_Annual, :Unc_Annual, :Anomaly_5yr, :Unc_5yr,
            :Anomaly_10yr, :Unc_10yr, :Anomaly_20yr, :Unc_20yr]
rename!(rawtemp, colnames)
rawtemp.Date = Date.(rawtemp.Year, rawtemp.Month, 1) # Create Date column from Year and Month
rename!(rawtemp, :Anomaly_Monthly => :RawTemperature)
berkeley = rawtemp[!, [:Date, :RawTemperature]]
oldbase = mean(rawtemp[(rawtemp.Date.>=Date(1850, 1, 1)).&(rawtemp.Date.<Date(1900, 1, 1)), :RawTemperature])
rawtemp[!, :Temp] = rawtemp[!, :RawTemperature] .- oldbase
berkeley.Temp = rawtemp.Temp
CSV.write(bfilename, berkeley)
last(berkeley, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the Berkeley Earth data
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview processed Berkeley Earth data
bfilename = "data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv"
berkeley = CSV.read(bfilename, DataFrame)
last(berkeley, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the Berkeley Earth data
```


## ERA5 {#sec-ERA5}

ERA5 is the fifth generation ECMWF reanalysis for the global climate and weather [@ERA5]. Data is available from 1940 in NetCDF format from the [Copernicus Climate Data Store](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/datasets/reanalysis-era5-single-levels-monthly-means?tab=overview). 

The API requires setting up a personal key in a `.cdsapirc` file, which should be placed in the root directory of the project. You can get a key by requesting it at the [Copernicus Climate Data Store website](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/). The code below assumes that you have already set up the `.cdsapirc` file with your key. The `CDSAPI` package is used to interact with the Copernicus Climate Data Store API and download the data.  To obtain the full dataset, the whole period from 1940 to 2026 is requested, but the file is large, close to 1.5 GB. For update purposes, the code can be modified to request a smaller period, for example, from 2020 to present. The code also specifies the variable to download (2m temperature), the time resolution (monthly), and the data format (NetCDF). 
 

```julia
# Download the ERA5 temperature data
dataset = "reanalysis-era5-single-levels-monthly-means"
request = """{
    "product_type": ["monthly_averaged_reanalysis"],
    "variable": ["2m_temperature"],
    "year": [
        "1940", "1941", "1942", "1943", "1944", "1945", "1946", "1947", "1948", "1949", 
        "1950", "1951", "1952", "1953", "1954", "1955", "1956", "1957", "1958", "1959",
        "1960", "1961", "1962", "1963", "1964", "1965", "1966", "1967", "1968", "1969",
        "1970", "1971", "1972", "1973", "1974", "1975", "1976", "1977", "1978", "1979",
        "1980", "1981", "1982", "1983", "1984", "1985", "1986", "1987", "1988", "1989",
        "1990", "1991", "1992", "1993", "1994", "1995", "1996", "1997", "1998", "1999",
        "2000", "2001", "2002", "2003", "2004", "2005", "2006", "2007", "2008", "2009",
        "2010", "2011", "2012", "2013", "2014", "2015", "2016", "2017", "2018", "2019",
        "2020", "2021", "2022", "2023", "2024", "2025", "2026"
    ],
    "month": [ "01", "02", "03", "04", "05", "06", "07", "08", "09", "10", "11", "12" ],
    "time": ["00:00"],
    "data_format": "netcdf",
    "download_format": "unarchived"
}"""
CDSAPI.retrieve(dataset, request, "data/era5_2m_temperature.nc")
```

Once the data is downloaded, we can use the `NCDatasets` package to read the NetCDF file and process the data. The code below reads the data, extracts the latitude, longitude, time, and temperature variables.

```julia
# Read ERA5 NetCDF metadata and time axis
filename = "data/era5_2m_temperature.nc"
ds = NCDataset(filename, "r")
lat_era = ds["latitude"][:]
time_era = ds["valid_time"][:]
date_era = Date.(time_era)
```

The next code snippet computes monthly global mean temperature from gridded ERA5 data. Because this step is computationally intensive, data are processed in chunks of 120 months to control memory use.

Latitude weights are based on the cosine of latitude to account for grid-cell area differences, so high-latitude cells receive less weight than equatorial cells. ERA5 values are reported in Kelvin and are converted to degrees Celsius.


```julia
# Calculate the global average temperature anomaly for each month
ini = 1
chunk_size = 120 # Number of months to skip for plotting
global_mean_C = zeros(length(date_era))
weights = cos.(lat_era * π / 180)
while ini <= length(date_era)
    last_idx = min(ini + chunk_size - 1, length(date_era))
    for i in ini:last_idx
        t2m = ds["t2m"][:, :, i]  # 2D slice [lon, lat] for first time; in K
        t_lon_mean = mean(t2m, dims=1)  # [lat]; drops new dim=2
        global_mean_K = sum(t_lon_mean' .* weights) / sum(weights)
        global_mean_C[i] = global_mean_K - 273.15
    end
    ini = ini + chunk_size
end
```

Once the monthly global mean temperatures are calculated, anomalies can be computed relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. Unlike the other datasets, ERA5 does not provide a fixed baseline period, so we first compute monthly climatology over 1991-2020 and then derive anomalies relative to that reference. Finally, we add an offset to approximate pre-industrial anomalies and save the processed data.^[The ERA5 pre-industrial offset (currently `0.88`) is obtained from the [ERA5 documentation](https://climate.copernicus.eu/climate-bulletin-about-data-and-analysis#Referenceperiodsandothertimerelateddefinitions).]


```julia
# Obtain temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline
time_dates = Date.(ds["valid_time"][:])  # If not already
start_1991 = findfirst(≥(Date(1991,1,1)), time_dates)
end_2020 = findfirst(≥(Date(2021,1,1)), time_dates) - 1
# Compute monthly climatology for 1991-2020
month_clim = zeros(12)
for m in 1:12
    idx = (month.(time_dates[start_1991:end_2020]) .== m)
    month_clim[m] = mean(global_mean_C[start_1991:end_2020][idx])
end
anom_1991_2020 = global_mean_C .- month_clim[month.(time_dates)]
offset_preind = 0.88  # Latest ERA5-to-1850-1900
pa_anom = anom_1991_2020 .+ offset_preind
era5 = DataFrame(Date=time_dates, RawTemperature=anom_1991_2020, Temp=pa_anom)
CSV.write("data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv", era5)
```

The snippet below reads the processed ERA5 data from the CSV file and shows the last 3 rows of the data.

```julia
# Read processed ERA5 table and preview recent values
erafilename = "data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv"
era5 = CSV.read(erafilename, DataFrame)
last(era5, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the ERA5 data
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview processed ERA5 data
efilename = "data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv"
era5 = CSV.read(efilename, DataFrame)
last(era5, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the ERA5 data
```

## Oceanic Niño Index (ONI) {#sec-ONI}

El Niño (La Niña) is identified when the 3-month running mean of sea surface temperature anomalies in the Niño 3.4 region remains above +0.5°C (below -0.5°C) for at least five consecutive overlapping seasons.

The data is obtained from the Extended Reconstructed Sea Surface Temperature (ERSST) dataset, which is a global monthly analysis of sea surface temperature data derived from the International Comprehensive Ocean–Atmosphere Dataset (ICOADS) [@SST_Part1; @SST_Part2]. The ONI data is available in text format from [the NOAA Climate Monitoring website](https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/access/monitoring/enso/) [@NOAA_ONI]. The ONI dataset is provided in a space-delimited format. The code below reads the data, processes it, and saves it in a CSV file. The last line shows the last 3 rows of the processed ONI data.

```julia
# Download the ONI data
ourl = "https://www.cpc.ncep.noaa.gov/data/indices/sstoi.indices"
ofilename = "data/Nino_data.csv"
open(ofilename, "w") do io
    write(io, HTTP.get(ourl).body)
end
lines = readlines(ofilename)
cleaned_lines = [join(split(strip(line)), ",") for line in lines]
# Write to file
write(ofilename, join(cleaned_lines, "\n"))
rawoni = CSV.read(ofilename, DataFrame; delim=',', header=1)
dates = Date.(rawoni.YR, rawoni.MON, 1) # Create Date column from YR and MON
oni = DataFrame(Date=dates, Anom=rawoni[!, :ANOM_3])
CSV.write(ofilename, oni)
last(oni, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the ONI data
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview processed ONI data
ofilename = "data/Nino_data.csv"
oni = CSV.read(ofilename, DataFrame)
last(oni, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the ONI data
```

The ONI dataset is now saved in the repository as [`Nino_data.csv`](data/Nino_data.csv). The data contains the date and the ONI anomaly values, which will be used to annotate El Niño and La Niña phases in the visualisations.

## Merge all datasets {#sec-merge-all-datasets}

Once all datasets are processed and saved as CSV files, we merge them into a single table for analysis and visualisation. The code below reads each dataset, merges them on `Date` with `leftjoin`, and renames columns for clarity. The resulting dataset contains temperature anomalies for each source alongside ONI. 


```julia
# Load the datasets
hadcrut = CSV.read("data/HadCRUT5_global_monthly_average.csv", DataFrame)
gistemp = CSV.read("data/GISTEMP_global_monthly_average.csv", DataFrame)
noaa = CSV.read("data/NOAA_global_monthly_average.csv", DataFrame)
berkeley = CSV.read("data/BerkeleyEarth_global_monthly_average.csv", DataFrame)
era5 = CSV.read("data/ERA5_global_monthly_average.csv", DataFrame)
oni = CSV.read("data/Nino_data.csv", DataFrame)
# Dates
min_date = minimum([minimum(hadcrut.Date), minimum(gistemp.Date), minimum(noaa.Date), minimum(berkeley.Date), minimum(oni.Date)])
max_date = maximum([maximum(hadcrut.Date), maximum(gistemp.Date), maximum(noaa.Date), maximum(berkeley.Date), maximum(oni.Date)])
complete_dates = collect(min_date:Month(1):max_date)
compiled_data = DataFrame(Date=complete_dates)
# Merge temperature datasets and standardise column names
merge_specs = [
    (hadcrut, :RawTemperature => :HadCRUT_RawTemperature, :Temp => :HadCRUT_Temp),
    (gistemp, :RawTemp => :GISTEMP_RawTemperature, :Temp => :GISTEMP_Temp),
    (noaa, :RawTemp => :NOAA_RawTemperature, :Temp => :NOAA_Temp),
    (berkeley, :RawTemperature => :Berkeley_RawTemperature, :Temp => :Berkeley_Temp),
    (era5, :RawTemperature => :ERA5_RawTemperature, :Temp => :ERA5_Temp),
]
for (df, raw_map, temp_map) in merge_specs
    compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, df, on=:Date)
    rename!(compiled_data, raw_map)
    rename!(compiled_data, temp_map)
end
# Merge ONI data
compiled_data = leftjoin(compiled_data, oni, on=:Date)
rename!(compiled_data, :Anom => :ONI_Anomaly)
sort!(compiled_data, :Date)
# Save the compiled data
compiled_filename = "data/Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv"
CSV.write(compiled_filename, compiled_data)
last(compiled_data, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the compiled data
```

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| output: true
# Preview merged dataset
afilename = "data/Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv"
compiled = CSV.read(afilename, DataFrame)
last(compiled, 3) # Show the last 3 rows of the compiled dataset
```

The compiled dataset is now saved in the repository as [`Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv`](data/Compiled_Global_Temperature_Data.csv). The data contains the date, raw temperature anomalies for each dataset, and the temperature anomalies relative to the 1850-1900 baseline. It also includes ONI anomalies. The merged dataset makes it easier to compare trends across datasets, assess progress towards the goals of the Paris Agreement, and annotate ENSO phases in the visualisations.

## Plot the data {#sec-plot-data}

Once the data are compiled, we can proceed to visualise them. The code below loads the compiled dataset and sets plot aesthetics using the `Plots` package. The `theme(:ggplot2)` function sets the overall style, while `default` sets the font family and sizes for ticks, legend, title, and axis labels. The x-axis ticks are shown every 180 months (15 years) for readability.

```julia
# Load the compiled data and plot packages
compiled_data = CSV.read(compiled_filename, DataFrame)
# Set plot aesthetics
theme(:ggplot2)
default(
    fontfamily = "Computer Modern", titlefontfamily = "Computer Modern", 
    legendfontfamily = "Computer Modern", tickfontfamily = "Computer Modern",
    tickfontsize = 10, legendfontsize = 10, titlefontsize = 12,
    xlabelfontsize = 10, ylabelfontsize = 10,
)
# Extract the dates for x-axis ticks
# This will be used for the x-axis ticks in the plot
xls = compiled_data.Date
```

The next chunk plots each dataset with a distinct marker shape and colour for clarity. The x-axis represents date (monthly), and the y-axis represents temperature anomaly in degrees Celsius.

```julia
# List of series specifications: (label, column name, marker shape)
series_specs = [
    ("HadCRUT5", :HadCRUT_Temp, :circle), ("GISTEMP", :GISTEMP_Temp, :diamond),
    ("NOAAGlobalTemp", :NOAA_Temp, :+), ("Berkeley Earth", :Berkeley_Temp, :xcross),
    ("ERA5", :ERA5_Temp, :star),
]
# Plotting the first temperature data
p = plot(
    compiled_data.Date,
    compiled_data[!, series_specs[1][2]],
    title="Global Temperature Anomalies", label=series_specs[1][1],
    xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)",
    linewidth=0.5, markershape=series_specs[1][3], markersize=1,
)
# Plotting the remaining temperature data
for (label, col, marker) in series_specs[2:end]
    plot!(compiled_data.Date, compiled_data[!, col], label=label, linewidth=0.5, markershape=marker, markersize=1)
end
# Set x-axis ticks every 180 months (15 years)
plot!(legend=:topleft, xticks=(xls[1:180:end], Dates.format.(xls[1:180:end], "Y"))) # Set x-axis ticks every 180 months
display(p)
```

![Global Temperature Anomalies](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf){width="80%"}

The plot shows that global temperature anomalies have increased over time, with a noticeable acceleration in recent decades. Furthermore, the plot indicates that the 1.5 °C threshold has already been exceeded in some months in recent years.

<!---
The plot is saved as a PDF file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf) and as a PNG file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies.png`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.png).

```julia
# Save the plot
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.pdf")
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies.png")
```
--->

### The last 30 years

To better visualise recent trends in global temperature anomalies, we zoom in on the last 30 years of data, from January 1995 to the present. The code below filters the compiled dataset and creates a new plot with the same aesthetics as before.

```julia
# Zoom in on the last 30 years of data
compiled_data_zoomed = compiled_data[compiled_data.Date .>= Date(1995, 1, 1), :]
# Plotting the zoomed-in data first dataset
p_zoomed = plot(
    compiled_data_zoomed.Date,
    compiled_data_zoomed[!, series_specs[1][2]],
    title="Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)", label=series_specs[1][1],
    xlabel="Date (monthly)", ylabel="Temperature Anomaly (°C)",
    linewidth=0.5, markershape=series_specs[1][3], markersize=1,
)
# Plotting the remaining temperature data
for (label, col, marker) in series_specs[2:end]
    plot!(compiled_data_zoomed.Date, compiled_data_zoomed[!, col], label=label, linewidth=0.5, markershape=marker, markersize=1)
end
# Set x-axis ticks every 60 months (5 years) for the zoomed plot
plot!(legend=:topleft, xticks=(compiled_data_zoomed.Date[1:60:end], Dates.format.(compiled_data_zoomed.Date[1:60:end], "Y")))
display(p_zoomed)
```

![Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years)](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf){width="80%"}

The figure shows periods of rapid warming superimposed on shorter-term fluctuations, many of which are associated with ENSO variability. To make this relationship explicit, the next section overlays El Niño and La Niña phases.

<!---
Similar to the previous plot, the figure can be saved as a PDF file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf) and as a PNG file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.png`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.png).

```julia
# Save the zoomed plot
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.pdf")
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years.png")
```
--->

### Adding El Niño and La Niña events {#sec-Adding-ONI}

To add the periods of El Niño and La Niña events to the plot, we will create an indicator variable based on the ONI anomalies. An El Niño event is defined as a period when the ONI anomaly is above +0.5°C, a La Niña event is defined as a period when the ONI anomaly is below -0.5°C, and a Neutral event is defined as a period when the ONI anomaly is between -0.5°C and +0.5°C. The code below classifies the ONI anomalies into these three categories and creates an indicator variable that takes the value of 1 for El Niño events, -1 for La Niña events, and 0 for Neutral events.

```julia
# Classify into El Niño, La Niña, and Neutral events
ONI_Anomaly = compiled_data_zoomed.ONI_Anomaly[.!ismissing.(compiled_data_zoomed.ONI_Anomaly)]
T = length(ONI_Anomaly)
indicator = zeros(Int, T)
# Classify ONI anomalies into El Niño, La Niña, and Neutral events
for i in 1:T
    if ONI_Anomaly[i] >= 0.5
        indicator[i] = 1
    elseif ONI_Anomaly[i] <= -0.5
        indicator[i] = -1
    else
        indicator[i] = 0
    end
end
```

Once we have defined the indicator variable, we can proceed to add the shaded areas to the plot for the identified El Niño and La Niña events. The code below iterates through the `indicator` array to identify the start and stop indices of El Niño and La Niña events. It uses the `vspan!` function to add vertical shaded areas to the plot for each identified event, with red shading for El Niño events and blue shading for La Niña events. The alpha parameter is set to 0.1 to make the shaded areas semi-transparent, allowing the temperature anomaly lines to remain visible. The bars are added to the existing plot `p_zoomed` which shows the global temperature anomalies for the last 30 years. Finally, the updated plot is displayed.

```julia
# Shade El Niño and La Niña intervals on the zoomed plot
p_zoomed_oni = p_zoomed
i = 1
while i <= length(indicator)
    current_val = indicator[i]
    if current_val in (-1, 1)
        start_idx = i
        while i <= length(indicator) && indicator[i] == current_val
            i = i + 1
        end
        stop_idx = i - 1
        if (stop_idx <= start_idx) || (stop_idx - start_idx < 4)
            continue
        else
            vspan!(p_zoomed_oni, [compiled_data_zoomed.Date[start_idx], compiled_data_zoomed.Date[stop_idx]], color=current_val == 1 ? :red : :blue, alpha=0.1, label ="")
        end
    else
        i = i + 1
    end
end
display(p_zoomed_oni)
```

![Global Temperature Anomalies (Last 30 Years) with ONI Events](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf){width="80%"}

The plot now shows global temperature anomalies over the last 30 years with shaded El Niño (red) and La Niña (blue) intervals based on ONI. This overlay clarifies how short-term temperature swings co-move with ENSO phases and improves interpretation of the underlying warming trend.

<!---

This last plot can also be saved as a PDF file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf) and as a PNG file as [`Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.png`](data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.png).

```julia
# Save the plot with ONI events
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.pdf")
savefig("data/Global_Temperature_Anomalies_Last_30_Years_ONI.png")
```
-->


## Conclusions

This paper provides a reproducible workflow for harmonising major global temperature anomaly datasets to a common 1850-1900 baseline and unified structure. The resulting dataset supports transparent cross-product comparison and consistent downstream analysis.

By combining harmonised anomaly series with ONI-based ENSO annotations, the framework separates short-term variability from the long-term warming signal. The approach is designed for reuse in climate services and policy-facing analysis, including tracking progress against Paris Agreement-related temperature targets. All code and processed datasets are openly published, supporting open-science practices and enabling independent verification and reuse across the research community. The annotated multi-dataset figures are designed for clear visual communication of temperature trends to both scientific and broader audiences.


::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}

### References {.unnumbered}

:::

::: {#refs}
:::

## Citation

If you use any of the data or code in this notebook, please cite the original datasets and this notebook as follows:

```bibtex
@article{vera-valdes2026,
  author = {Vera-Valdés, J. Eduardo},
    title = {Global Temperature Anomalies in Practice: An Open, Reproducible Framework for Baseline Harmonisation and ENSO-Aware Visualisation},
  journal = {Submitted to Visualising Climate 2026}
}
```
